title: High-Level Modeling Functions with torch
text: R brulee package contains several basic modeling functions use torch package infrastructure, : neural networks linear regression logistic regression multinomial regression residual networks (ResNet) regularization learning networks (RLN) AutoInt Self-Attention Inter-sample Attention Transformer (Saint) Chronos2 foundational model forecasting Chronos2 model uses pretrained model, requiring one-time download 500MB.

title: High-Level Modeling Functions with torch
text: brulee formula, x/y, recipe user interfaces function. example: recipe can also used data require sort preprocessing (e.g., indicator variables, transformations, standardization):
code: library(brulee) library(recipes) library(yardstick)  data(bivariate, package = "modeldata") set.seed(20) nn_log_biv <- brulee_mlp(Class ~ log(A) + log(B), data = bivariate_train,                           epochs = 150, hidden_units = 3)  # We use the tidymodels semantics to always return a tibble when predicting predict(nn_log_biv, bivariate_test, type = "prob") |>    bind_cols(bivariate_test) |>    roc_auc(Class, .pred_One) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   .metric .estimator .estimate #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> #> 1 roc_auc binary         0.840 library(recipes)  rec <-    recipe(Class ~ ., data = bivariate_train) |>     step_YeoJohnson(all_numeric_predictors()) |>    step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())  set.seed(20) nn_rec_biv <- brulee_mlp(rec, data = bivariate_train,                           epochs = 150, hidden_units = 3)  # A little better predict(nn_rec_biv, bivariate_test, type = "prob") |>    bind_cols(bivariate_test) |>    roc_auc(Class, .pred_One) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   .metric .estimator .estimate #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> #> 1 roc_auc binary         0.862

text: AutoInt architecture three stages: Embedding layer: Maps every feature (categorical continuous) shared vector space dimension num_embedding. Self-attention backbone: stack num_attn_blocks multi-head self-attention layers. blocks, residual connection original embeddings added activation applied. code: # \donttest{ if (torch::torch_is_installed()  & rlang::is_installed(c("recipes", "yardstick", "modeldata"))) {   ## -----------------------------------------------------------------------------   library(recipes)  library(yardstick)   data(ames, package = "modeldata")   ames$Sale_Price <- log10(ames$Sale_Price)   set.seed(122)  in_train <- sample(1:nrow(ames), 2000)  ames_train <- ames[ in_train,]  ames_test  <- ames[-in_train,]    # Using matrices  set.seed(1)  brulee_linear_reg(x = as.matrix(ames_train[, c("Longitude", "Latitude")]),                     y = ames_train$Sale_Price,                     penalty = 0.10, epochs = 1, batch_size = 64)   # Using recipe  library(recipes)   ames_rec <-   recipe(Sale_Price ~ Bldg_Type + Neighborhood + Year_Built + Gr_Liv_Area +          Full_Bath + Year_Sold + Lot_Area + Central_Air + Longitude + Latitude,          data = ames_train) |>     # Transform some highly skewed predictors     step_BoxCox(Lot_Area, Gr_Liv_Area) |>     # Lump some rarely occurring categories into "other"     step_other(Neighborhood, threshold = 0.05)  |>     # Encode categorical predictors as binary.     step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors(), one_hot = TRUE) |>     # Add an interaction effect:     step_interact(~ starts_with("Central_Air"):Year_Built) |>     step_zv(all_predictors()) |>     step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())   set.seed(2)  fit <- brulee_linear_reg(ames_rec, data = ames_train, epochs = 5)  fit   autoplot(fit)   library(ggplot2)   predict(fit, ames_test) |>    bind_cols(ames_test) |>    ggplot(aes(x = .pred, y = Sale_Price)) +    geom_abline(col = "green") +    geom_point(alpha = .3) +    lims(x = c(4, 6), y = c(4, 6)) +    coord_fixed(ratio = 1)   library(yardstick)  predict(fit, ames_test) |>    bind_cols(ames_test) |>    rmse(Sale_Price, .pred)   } #> Warning: `batch_size` is only used for the SGD optimizer. #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   .metric .estimator .estimate #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> #> 1 rmse    standard      0.0821  # }

Add an interaction effect:    step_interact(~ starts_with("Central_Air"):Year_Built) |>    step_zv(all_predictors()) |>    step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())   set.seed(2)  fit <- brulee_mlp(ames_rec, data = ames_train, hidden_units = 20,                     dropout = 0.05, rate_schedule = "cyclic", step_size = 4)  fit   autoplot(fit)   library(ggplot2)   predict(fit, ames_test) |>    bind_cols(ames_test) |>    ggplot(aes(x = .pred, y = Sale_Price)) +    geom_abline(col = "green") +    geom_point(alpha = .3) +    lims(x = c(4, 6), y = c(4, 6)) +    coord_fixed(ratio = 1)   library(yardstick)  predict(fit, ames_test) |>    bind_cols(ames_test) |>    rmse(Sale_Price, .pred)   # Using multiple hidden layers and activation functions  set.seed(2)  hidden_fit <- brulee_mlp(ames_rec, data = ames_train,                     hidden_units = c(15L, 17L), activation = c("relu", "elu"),                     dropout = 0.05, rate_schedule = "cyclic", step_size = 4)   predict(hidden_fit, ames_test) |>    bind_cols(ames_test) |>    rmse(Sale_Price, .pred)   # ------------------------------------------------------------------------------  # classification   library(dplyr)  library(ggplot2)   data("parabolic", package = "modeldata")   set.seed(1)  in_train <- sample(1:nrow(parabolic), 300)  parabolic_tr <- parabolic[ in_train,]  parabolic_te <- parabolic[-in_train,]   set.seed(2)  cls_fit <- brulee_mlp(class ~ ., data = parabolic_tr, hidden_units = 2,                         epochs = 200L, learn_rate = 0.1, activation = "elu",                         penalty = 0.1, batch_size = 2^8, optimizer = "SGD")   summary(cls_fit)   autoplot(cls_fit)   grid_points <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 100)   grid <- expand.grid(X1 = grid_points, X2 = grid_points)   predict(cls_fit, grid, type = "prob") |>   bind_cols(grid) |>   ggplot(aes(X1, X2)) +   geom_contour(aes(z = .pred_Class1), breaks = 1/2, col = "black") +   geom_point(data = parabolic_te, aes(col = class))   } #> Multilayer perceptron architecture #> inputs: 2 | output dim: 2 | components: 3 #>  #>   Linear(2 -> 2)                        6 params #>   ELU                                   0 params #>   Linear(2 -> 2)                        6 params #>  #> Total parameters: 12  # }

title: Fit Regularization Learning Networks (RLN) — brulee_rln
text: RLNs designed tabular datasets interpretability matters. per-weight regularization tends produce sparse networks. original paper reports eliminating ~99.8% network edges ~82% input features. sparsity makes easier identify inputs network considers important, resulting models competitive gradient boosted trees. best results paper achieved ensembling RLNs gradient boosting tree ensembles.

text: brulee_saint() fits SAINT (Self-Attention Inter-sample Attention Transformer) model Somepalli et al (2021). SAINT applies multi-head self-attention across features (column attention) samples within batch (row/inter-sample attention) learn complex feature interactions.

title: Fit SAINT models for tabular data — brulee_saint
text: SAINT architecture three stages: Embedding layer: Categorical features mapped per-feature embedding tables. Continuous features passed per-feature MLPs (1 -> 100 -> num_embedding). initial embeddings per-feature; distinct embedding MLP predictor. Also, see "Target Token Pooling" section . Transformer backbone: stack num_attn_blocks transformer layers. layer contains multi-head self-attention followed feed-forward network GeGLU activation. attention_type = "", block alternates column attention (across features) row attention (across samples within batch). Output head: Pools transformer output (either target token's embedding flattened concatenation feature embeddings, controlled use_target_token) projects optional hidden layers output dimension. summary() methods can provide details architecture specific model fit. Differences implementation original paper: pretraining supported.

title: Fit SAINT models for tabular data — brulee_saint
text: Column attention ("column"): Standard self-attention features. feature embedding attends feature embeddings. Row attention ("row"): inter-sample attention. Reshapes batch sample's full feature representation becomes single token, applies attention across samples batch. (""): Alternates column row attention transformer block. full SAINT model.

title: Change the learning rate over time — schedule_decay_time
code: schedule_decay_time(epoch, initial = 0.1, decay = 1)  schedule_decay_expo(epoch, initial = 0.1, decay = 1)  schedule_step(epoch, initial = 0.1, reduction = 1/2, steps = 5)  schedule_cyclic(epoch, initial = 0.001, largest = 0.1, step_size = 5)  set_learn_rate(epoch, learn_rate, type = "none", ...)

title: Change the learning rate over time — schedule_decay_time
text: epoch integer number training epochs (zero initial value), initial positive numeric value starting learning rate. decay positive numeric constant decreasing rate (see Details ). reduction positive numeric constant stating proportional decrease learning rate occurring every steps epochs. steps number epochs learning rate changes. largest maximum learning rate cycle. step_size half-length cycle. learn_rate constant learning rate (scheduler used), type single character value type scheduler. Possible values : "decay_time", "decay_expo", "none", "cyclic", "step". ... Arguments pass individual scheduler functions (e.g. reduction).

title: Change the learning rate over time — schedule_decay_time
text: numeric value updated learning rate.

title: Change the learning rate over time — schedule_decay_time
text: details schedulers change rates: schedule_decay_time(): \(rate(epoch) = initial/(1 + decay \times epoch)\) schedule_decay_expo(): \(rate(epoch) = initial\exp(-decay \times epoch)\) schedule_step(): \(rate(epoch) = initial \times reduction^{floor(epoch / steps)}\) schedule_cyclic(): \(cycle = floor( 1 + (epoch / 2 / step size) )\), \(x = abs( ( epoch / step size ) - ( 2 * cycle) + 1 )\), \(rate(epoch) = initial + ( largest - initial ) * \max( 0, 1 - x)\)

title: Change the learning rate over time — schedule_decay_time
code: if (rlang::is_installed("purrr")) {  library(ggplot2)  library(dplyr)  library(purrr)   iters <- 0:50   bind_rows(   tibble(epoch = iters, rate = map_dbl(iters, schedule_decay_time), type = "decay_time"),   tibble(epoch = iters, rate = map_dbl(iters, schedule_decay_expo), type = "decay_expo"),   tibble(epoch = iters, rate = map_dbl(iters, schedule_step), type = "step"),   tibble(epoch = iters, rate = map_dbl(iters, schedule_cyclic), type = "cyclic")  ) |>   ggplot(aes(epoch, rate)) +   geom_line() +   facet_wrap(~ type)  }

title: brulee (development version)
text: brulee_saint() brulee_auto_int() now support gradient clipping via grad_value_clip grad_norm_clip arguments (default 5), matching brulee_mlp() brulee_resnet(). prevents loss overflowing NaN training aggressive learning rates.

title: brulee 1.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-06-17 New models tabular data: Regularization Learning Networks (brulee_rln()) use conventional MLP architecture weight learns adaptive regularization coefficient. ResNet (brulee_resnet()) can fit multilayer neural network skip (.e. residual) connections batch normalization. AutoInt (brulee_auto_int()) uses residual connections columnwise attention mechanisms create embeddings encourage -context learning features. Saint (brulee_saint()) uses column /row attention mechanisms. Chronos2 (brulee_chronos()) foundational model forecasting. modeling functions now support GPU acceleration via device parameter. Users can specify device = "cpu", device = "cuda", device = "mps" (Apple Silicon). device = NULL (default), package automatically selects CUDA available, otherwise defaults CPU. Note: MPS auto-selected doesn’t support float64 dtype required brulee. See?training_efficiency related notes.

title: brulee 1.0.0
text: Float tensors changed 64-bit floats 32-bit. enable GPU usage MPS devices. Parameters initialized CPU devices converted chosen device. cases, RNG initialization code independent seed. classification, softmax moved every model’s forward pass loss can use torch::nnf_cross_entropy() (applies log-sum-exp trick internally) instead nll_loss(log(softmax(x))). avoids log(0) underflow produced NaN losses “numerical overflow” early stopping overspecified brulee_saint() / brulee_auto_int() fits. Affects brulee_mlp(), brulee_logistic_reg(), brulee_multinomial_reg(), brulee_resnet(), brulee_auto_int(), brulee_saint(). New fits carry output_type = "logits" predict path applies softmax; serialized fits earlier versions brulee continue predict correctly.

title: brulee 0.6.0
text: CRAN release: 2025-09-02 Transition magrittr pipe base R pipe. try help avoiding numeric overflow loss functions: Tensors stored 64-bit float instead 32-bit. Starting values transitioned using Gaussian distribution (instead uniform) smaller standard deviation. results always contain initial results use fallback overflow first epoch. brulee_mlp() two additional parameters, grad_value_clip grad_value_clip, prevent issues. warning changed “Early stopping occurred epoch {X} due numerical overflow loss function.” Several new SGD optimizers added: "ADAMw", "Adadelta", "Adagrad", "RMSprop". Mixture parameter values different zero used several optimizers since require L2 penalties.

title: brulee 0.5.0
text: CRAN release: 2025-04-07 Removed unit test numerical overflow since occurs less frequently become increasingly challenging reproduce.

title: brulee 0.4.0
text: CRAN release: 2025-01-30 Added convenience function, brulee_mlp_two_layer(), easily fit two-layer networks parsnip. Various changes improvements error warning messages. Fixed bug occurred linear activation used neural networks (#68).

title: brulee 0.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2024-02-14 Fixed bug coef() didn’t error used brulee_logistic_reg() trained recipe. (#66) Fixed bug SGD always used optimizer (#61). Additional activation functions added (#74).

title: brulee 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2022-09-19 Several learning rate schedulers added modeling functions (#12). optimizer added [brulee_mlp()], new default LBFGS instead stochastic gradient descent.

title: brulee 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2022-02-02 Modeling functions gained mixture argument proportion L1 penalty used. (#50) Penalization occurring quasi-Newton optimization chosen. (#50)
